title: Sphere Packings, Spectral Gaps and the Conformal Bootstrap
speaker: Dalimil Mazac
affiliation: Institut de Physique Théorique - CEA Paris-Saclay
abstract: I will discuss infinite-dimensional linear programs producing bounds on the spectral gap in various settings. This includes new bounds on the spectral gap of hyperbolic manifolds as well as the Cohn+Elkies bound on the density of sphere packings. The bounds allow us to essentially determine the complete set of spectral gaps achieved by hyperbolic 2-orbifolds. The linear programs involved have been the subject of intense study by mathematical physicists in the context of the conformal bootstrap.

proof_stages.label: Linear-program spectral-bound template
proof_stages.summary: The talk is framed around a common scheme: derive spectral identities, translate them into infinite-dimensional linear or semidefinite programs, extract spectral-gap bounds, and in favorable cases prove sharpness.

proof_stages.label: Hyperbolic-orbifold spectral identities
proof_stages.summary: For a compact hyperbolic two-orbifold, the Laplace spectrum is related to holomorphic modular-form data by comparing two decompositions of a quadruple product in L^2(Gamma\G), with uniqueness of PSL(2,R) trilinear functionals fixing the hypergeometric spectral terms.

proof_stages.label: Positivity-to-gap linear program
proof_stages.summary: Expanding the spectral identities gives positive weighted polynomial sums in Laplace eigenvalues, and suitable sign constraints on linear combinations of these polynomials force an eigenvalue below a chosen lambda*, yielding computable upper bounds and near-reconstruction of spectra when the bound is close to sharp.

proof_stages.label: Topological refinements for orbifold gaps
proof_stages.summary: Using only topological information about the dimensions and weights of holomorphic modular forms, the method gives fixed-genus bounds and refined class-by-class bounds that almost determine the set of possible first eigenvalues of compact hyperbolic two-orbifolds.

proof_stages.label: Poisson and crossing as parallel linear programs
proof_stages.summary: The Fourier-odd Cohn-Elkies problem for isodual lattices and the one-dimensional conformal-bootstrap problem are recast in the same form: maximize the first spectral point subject to a positive spectral expansion invariant under an involution.

proof_stages.label: Modular-lambda bridge
proof_stages.summary: The modular lambda function identifies the bootstrap crossing transformation z->1-z with the modular S-transform tau->-1/tau, while T-eigenvalues impose d=16p and a positive expansion of Gaussians into hypergeometric blocks explains why the transfer is exact in the 8- and 24-dimensional cases.

proof_stages.label: Extremal functional and magic-function construction
proof_stages.summary: Sharpness of the bootstrap bound is proved by first exhibiting the admissible arithmetic spectrum and then constructing an extremal linear functional with prescribed double zeros, whose action on hypergeometric blocks gives the bootstrap magic function and whose action on Gaussians recovers the sphere-packing magic and interpolation functions.

proof_stages.label: General-p kernels and extensions
proof_stages.summary: The contour-kernel construction is extended to general p through functional equations and interpolation bases, with later developments addressing deformed double-zero locations, finer spectral-distribution questions, and higher-dimensional conformal-bootstrap analogues for SO(n,2).

examples.name: Bolza surface near the genus-two bound
examples.note: The Bolza surface is cited as a highly symmetric genus-two surface whose computed first eigenvalue nearly saturates the linear-program upper bound, serving as numerical evidence for sharpness.
examples.engagement_reason: the Bolza surface is cited as nearly saturating the genus-two numerical bound, but no calculation or construction is performed on it

examples.name: Noncompact orbifolds and Eisenstein series
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that the same representation-theoretic identity would acquire a continuous Eisenstein contribution for noncompact orbifolds, illustrating the compactness assumption and why the stated bounds are not useful below the usual continuous-spectrum threshold.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only notes that for noncompact orbifolds a continuous Eisenstein-series contribution would enter the left-hand side, without carrying out the method on a specific noncompact case

passes.reason: Standard noncompact orbifolds have Eisenstein continuous spectrum, used here as an obstruction to extending the compact spectral-gap argument.
noncompact orbifolds introduce Eisenstein/continuous-spectrum terms that obstruct the compact spectral-gap argument
Ordinary cusped/noncompact orbifolds with Eisenstein continuous spectrum are invoked as an obstruction to useful compact-style λ1 bounds.
Noncompact orbifolds with Eisenstein continuous spectrum are ordinary cusp cases, but are invoked as an obstruction to extending the compact spectral-gap argument.
cusped noncompact orbifolds with Eisenstein continuous spectrum are used as an obstruction to the compact spectral-gap program rather than as a sporadic or extremal object
noncompact orbifolds add Eisenstein continuous spectrum, serving as an obstruction to extracting new compact-style λ1 bounds
Cusped noncompact orbifolds introduce Eisenstein continuous spectrum that obstructs useful λ1 bounds, while forming a standard noncompact family.
Noncompact orbifolds with Eisenstein continuous spectrum are invoked as an obstruction to getting useful compact-style lambda_1 bounds, but they are not structurally sporadic or extremal.
Cusped noncompact orbifolds bring Eisenstein continuous spectrum that obstructs the compact spectral-gap argument.
the noncompact cusped case brings in Eisenstein continuous spectrum and obstructs a direct useful λ1 bound, but it is a standard family rather than structurally special
Cusped noncompact orbifolds introduce Eisenstein continuous spectrum that obstructs the compact spectral-gap argument, but are otherwise standard objects.
The noncompact case is raised as an obstruction because Eisenstein continuous spectrum spoils the compact spectral-gap argument, while the objects are otherwise ordinary cusp orbifolds/series.

examples.name: The first Rankin-Cohen identity
examples.note: The special case m=1 gives a positive-weighted sum of a linear polynomial in the Laplace eigenvalue, visibly producing the sign-change idea that is then systematized into polynomial and linear-program bounds.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the odd Rankin-Cohen vanishing at m=1 to write the concrete spectral identity with a factor like lambda minus m and infer constraints on eigenvalues

passes.reason: The first Rankin-Cohen identity is the lowest vanishing/base case in the identity family, used illustratively rather than adversarially.
the m=1 Rankin-Cohen relation is the first vanishing odd-bracket base case used to get a simple spectral identity

examples.name: Dimension-two sphere-packing mismatch
examples.note: An audience question about dimension two lets the speaker clarify that the modular-lambda/bootstrapping match works only when the relevant spectrum fits the required arithmetic pattern, unlike the two-dimensional packing problem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks the dimension-two case against the bootstrap/sphere-packing correspondence and explains why the arithmetic-spectrum matching that works in dimensions 8 and 24 fails there

passes.reason: The two-dimensional sphere-packing case is used to show the bootstrap-to-sphere-packing matching fails outside the arithmetic-spectrum cases.
the two-dimensional optimal packing is the symmetric extremal hexagonal/A2 case, but its non-arithmetic spectrum is used as a mismatch obstructing the bootstrap mapping
The two-dimensional hexagonal/A2 packing is the optimal planar packing, but here it is used to show the bootstrap arithmetic-spectrum matching fails in dimension two.
